s: Europe's oldest deaf school, birthplace of Polish sign language
w: A Piarist priest named Jakub Falkowski founded this school in 1817, one of the first in Europe dedicated to deaf children. Set in a solemn neoclassical pile at Plac Trzech Krzyzy 4/6, it shaped Polish sign language from the start. Still a school. Still on the same square.
_facts: Founded 1817 by Jakub Falkowski, a Piarist priest, as one of the earliest deaf education institutions in Europe.
Located at Plac Trzech Krzyzy 4/6, Warsaw, in a neoclassical building.
Made major contributions to the development and standardisation of Polish sign language.
Named Instytut Głuchoniemych im. Jakuba Falkowskiego; still an active educational institution.

s: Warsaw's oldest surviving Jewish cemetery, wild and largely unreached
w: Warsaw had many Jewish cemeteries; this one in Targowek, opened 1780, is among the least visited and most overgrown. Five hectares of tilted matzevot swallowed by weeds and oak trees, a world unrestored and unreachable except on foot. Quietly extraordinary.
_facts: Founded in 1780, making it one of the oldest Jewish cemeteries in Warsaw.
Located in the Targowek district (Brodno area), covering 5 hectares.
Also known as Bródno Jewish Cemetery; distinct from the Catholic Cmentarz Bródnowski.
Largely unrestored; contains tombstones dating from the late 18th century.

s: Arthouse cinema standing on the rubble of the Warsaw Ghetto
w: An arthouse cinema at Andersa 5, in the heart of the Muranow estate built on the rubble of the Warsaw Ghetto. Poles watch Polish and foreign films on the same ground where hundreds of thousands lived before the war. One of the most quietly haunted cinemas in Europe.
_facts: Located at ul. gen. Władysława Andersa 5, Warsaw, Srodmiescie district.
Situated within the Muranow estate, built after WWII on the ruins of the Warsaw Ghetto.
Functions as an arthouse and independent cinema, programming Polish and international films.
The name Muranow derives from the pre-war Jewish quarter that once stood on this ground.

s: A 14th-century village church that Warsaw's tower blocks forgot to swallow
w: Tucked into the Stary Sluzew village green at Fosa 17, this Gothic brick church dates to the 14th century -- old when Warsaw was young, surviving on a modest scale the city forgot to demolish. A medieval remnant in a sea of 1970s tower blocks. Almost invisible, very old.
_facts: Located at ul. Fosa 17, Stary Sluzew, Ursynow district of Warsaw.
Gothic brick construction dating to the 14th century; one of the oldest surviving churches in the Warsaw area.
The village of Sluzew predates Warsaw itself; the church was the heart of that original settlement.
Surrounded by post-war housing estates, creating a striking contrast with its medieval scale and materials.

s: The 1920s housing cooperative where Warsaw's press corps moved in
w: In the 1920s Warsaw's journalists pooled money into a housing cooperative and built this compact functionalist quarter near Krasinskiego. The streets -- including one named Dziennikarska -- still read like an inter-war press club frozen in brick. Restrained, austere, perfect.
_facts: Zoliborz Dziennikarski (Journalists' Quarter) is an inter-war cooperative housing estate in northern Warsaw.
Built in the late 1920s and 1930s for members of a journalists' housing cooperative.
Located in Stary Zoliborz, bounded roughly by Krasinskiego, Sulkowskiego, Karpinskego and Dziennikarskiej streets.
The functionalist architecture typifies Warsaw's progressive inter-war workers' cooperative housing movement.

s: Monument to 40,000-50,000 civilians killed here in eight days, August 1944
w: Between 5 and 12 August 1944, SS and police units killed an estimated 40,000 to 50,000 civilians in Wola -- Himmler's order was to shoot anything that moved. This stark memorial near Leszno Street stands where the slaughter was densest. The largest urban massacre of the war.
_facts: Located near Leszno Street in Wola district, Warsaw.
Commemorates the Wola massacre of 5-12 August 1944, in which German SS and police killed 40,000-50,000 Polish civilians.
The massacre was ordered by Heinrich Himmler as collective punishment to suppress the Warsaw Uprising.
Perpetrators included SS-Sonderregiment Dirlewanger and Azerbaijani Legion units.
Generally considered the largest single urban massacre of the Second World War.

s: Soviet-designed coal plant, first turbine lit on Polish National Day 1954
w: Built between 1952 and 1956 to Soviet design specifications, its first turbine fired on 21 July 1954 -- Polish National Day, naturally. The four chimneys have dominated the Zeran skyline ever since. Still working, still steaming, still unmistakably Stalin-era infrastructure.
_facts: Located in the Zeran suburb of northeastern Warsaw (Bialoleka district).
Construction began 1952; first turbine became operational 21 July 1954, Polish National Day.
Built to Soviet design specifications; heat capacity of 1,561 MW, electric capacity 350 MW.
Underwent modernisation 1997-2001 when acquired by Vattenfall; now owned by PGNiG.
The four chimneys are visible across a wide arc of northern Warsaw.

s: The brick powerhouse that once kept Warsaw's tram network running
w: The red-brick power station at Przyokopowa 28 fed Warsaw's tram network from the early 20th century. The building survives in Wola, wedged between new developments, a handsome industrial remnant from the era when trams lit up the city before it was all flattened. Still standing.
_facts: Located at ul. Przyokopowa 28, Czyste, Wola district, Warsaw.
Formerly the Elektrownia Tramwajow Miejskich (Municipal Tram Power Station).
Built in the early 20th century to supply electricity to Warsaw's expanding tram network.
The red-brick industrial building survives among newer developments in Wola.

s: Warsaw's longest-running cinema, 1938-2014, now a supermarket
w: From 1938 to 2014, Femina at Aleja Solidarnosci 115 was Warsaw's oldest continuously operating picture house -- a narrow functionalist building that outlasted the war, communism and the multiplex. A Biedronka supermarket now occupies the ground floor. Flowers were left for weeks.
_facts: Kino Femina operated at Aleja Solidarnosci 115, Warsaw, from 1938 to 2014.
It was the longest continuously operating cinema in Warsaw at the time of its closure.
The functionalist building dates to 1937 and survived WWII and the communist period.
Closed in 2014; the building now houses a Biedronka supermarket on the ground floor.
Its closure was widely mourned by Warsaw's film community and film critics.

s: The Praga terminus named for a Vilnius that once felt very close
w: Named for its past as the gateway to trains bound for Vilnius, this Praga terminal at Targowa once faced east toward the old Polish-Lithuanian world. The current Stalinist building went up in the 1950s. Cross the Vistula from here and you have already crossed something else.
_facts: Located at ul. Targowa, Stara Praga, Praga-Polnoc district, Warsaw.
Its name derives from its historical role as the main Warsaw station for trains towards Vilnius.
The current Stalinist-era building was constructed in the 1950s.
Situated by Plac Wilenski on the right bank of the Vistula, marking the eastern entrance to Warsaw.

s: The University of Warsaw's 1820 observatory, hidden behind its own walls
w: Set in a small walled garden off Aleje Ujazdowskie, this compact neoclassical observatory dates from 1820. The University of Warsaw built it when astronomy still counted as prestige infrastructure. The domes are small. The building is lovely. The silence inside the gate is real.
_facts: Located at Aleje Ujazdowskie 4, Ujazdow, Srodmiescie, Warsaw.
Founded in 1820 as part of Warsaw University's scientific programme.
The neoclassical building sits in a walled garden largely invisible from the street.
Still operates as part of the Astronomical Observatory of Warsaw University (OA UW).

s: Where the world's largest synagogue stood until Stroop blew it up in 1943
w: At Tlomackie 3/5, on 16 May 1943, SS-General Jurgen Stroop detonated the Great Synagogue -- completed 1878 as the largest Jewish house of worship in the world. The explosion was intended to signal the end of the Ghetto Uprising. A blue glass tower fills the site today.
_facts: The Great Synagogue of Warsaw stood at Tlomackie street; designed by Leandro Marconi.
Completed 1878 in Neoclassical style; at its opening it was the largest Jewish house of worship in the world.
Deliberately blown up by SS-General Jurgen Stroop on 16 May 1943 to signal the end of the Warsaw Ghetto Uprising.
The site is now occupied by a modern blue glass office tower.
The Emanuel Ringelblum Jewish Historical Institute stands at the adjacent building.

s: A Wola cemetery turned mass killing site in August 1944
w: The Wola massacre unfolded across this district in the first week of August 1944; tens of thousands of bodies were brought here to Wolska 180. The Catholic cemetery was turned into a killing site. Simple wooden crosses mark mass graves among the older headstones. Go slowly.
_facts: Located at ul. Wolska 180/182, Ulrychow, Wola district, Warsaw.
Catholic cemetery established before WWII; location at the heart of the Wola massacre zone.
During the Wola massacre of 5-12 August 1944, German forces used the cemetery as a site for mass executions and burial.
Contains mass graves from the massacre alongside regular pre-war and post-war burials.
A low-profile but historically significant memorial site.

s: A 1780s palace that has served every Warsaw regime as a seat of power
w: Built in the 1780s for the Hilzen family, rebuilt in 1823 by minister Tytus Mostowski, this neoclassical palace at Nowolipie 2 has housed Russian governors, Polish ministers, archivists, and now the Warsaw Police. Two hundred years of authority, passed through the same gates.
_facts: Located at ul. Nowolipie 2, Muranow, Srodmiescie, Warsaw.
Originally built in the 1780s for the Hilzen family; rebuilt and expanded in 1823.
Named for Tytus Mostowski, Polish minister who commissioned its reconstruction.
Has served as seat of Russian imperial authorities, Polish ministries, archives, and is currently Warsaw Police HQ (Komenda Stoleczna Policji).
Listed neoclassical monument.
